skill.name: higgsfield-generate
skill.description: Generate images and videos using Higgsfield AI models. Supports GPT Image 2, Seedance 2.0, Marketing Studio for ads, Soul V2/Cinema/Cast/Location, Kling 3.0, and Virality Predictor for video scoring. Use for image generation, video creation, image-to-video, editing, ad creation, UGC video, product demo, and brand video. Chain with higgsfield-soul-id for face consistency across outputs.
skill.summary: Generate AI images, videos, and ads via the Higgsfield CLI with 30+ models including Marketing Studio and Virality Predictor.
skill.author: higgsfield-ai

security_audit.summary: All skill files are Markdown documentation (.md) containing CLI command examples. The static scanner produced 849 findings but all are false positives caused by misinterpreting Markdown code formatting as executable code patterns. Backticks in Markdown are inline code syntax, not Ruby shell execution. The curl-to-sh bootstrap pattern at SKILL.md:39 is a standard CLI installation convention from the project's own GitHub repository. No malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or data theft vectors were found. The skill legitimately wraps the official higgsfield CLI for image and video generation.

medium_findings.title: Ruby/shell backtick execution in Markdown files
medium_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static scanner flagged backtick usage across all 12 Markdown files as Ruby shell backtick execution. In Markdown files, backticks denote inline code formatting, not shell execution. All flagged lines contain CLI command examples shown in code blocks (e.g., `higgsfield generate create ...`) for user reference. These are documentation that instructs the AI agent, not Ruby scripts. 756 locations flagged, all false positives.

medium_findings.title: Shell command substitution in Markdown
medium_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static scanner flagged $(...) patterns as shell command substitution. These appear inside Markdown code blocks as documented CLI usage examples (e.g., `UPLOAD_ID=$(higgsfield upload create ...)`). The dollar-paren syntax is shown as a usage pattern for readers to understand, not executed code. No active shell injection risk.

medium_findings.title: Pipe to shell bootstrap pattern
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE (low severity). SKILL.md:39 contains `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/higgsfield-ai/cli/main/install.sh | sh`. This is a curl-to-sh bootstrap pattern for installing the higgsfield CLI. While standard in developer tooling, this pattern carries inherent risk as it downloads and executes a remote script. However, the URL points to the skill author's own GitHub repository and this is a documented one-time setup step. Risk is low but worth documenting as a known pattern.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm in Markdown
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static scanner flagged 20+ lines as 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. The lines include Markdown headings like '# Marketing Studio Modes' (line 1), table formatting characters ('---', '|'), and CLI flag descriptions. No cryptographic operations exist in these documentation files. The scanner likely confused Markdown syntax elements with base64 encoding or cryptographic patterns.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance commands in documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static scanner flagged CLI commands like `higgsfield account status`, `higgsfield model list`, and `higgsfield marketing-studio avatars list` as 'system reconnaissance'. These are standard CLI discovery commands for the Higgsfield platform, used to check authentication status and list available resources. This is normal operation for any CLI-based skill, not reconnaissance behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in documentation examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static scanner flagged example URLs in documentation. These are demonstration URLs (e.g., `https://drinkolipop.com`, `https://shop.example.com/sneakers`, `https://cdn/screenshot1.png`) shown in Markdown code blocks to illustrate CLI usage. No actual network calls are made by these URLs; they are documentation examples only.

low_findings.title: Temp file creation in code examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static scanner flagged `mktemp` invocations in code examples as temporary file creation. These appear in documented CLI workflows where temp files are needed to pass JSON arrays (e.g., `AVATARS_JSON=$(mktemp)`). This is proper bash practice for creating temporary files, not a security concern.

low_findings.title: Fetch API and network calls in Markdown examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static scanner flagged `fet` and network-related patterns in reference files. These are documented CLI command examples (e.g., `higgsfield marketing-studio products fetch --url ...`) that the AI agent would execute through the CLI, not raw HTTP requests from the skill code. The skill correctly routes all API calls through the official higgsfield CLI as specified in project conventions.

content.user_title: Generate AI images, videos and ads with Higgsfield
content.value_statement: Marketers and creators need fast, consistent visual content production without complex design tools. This skill connects you to 30+ Higgsfield AI models through simple text commands to generate images, videos, branded ads, and marketing content.
actual_capabilities: Generate images using GPT Image 2, Nano Banana 2, Soul 2.0, and 20+ other image models
Create videos with Seedance 2.0, Kling 3.0, Marketing Studio, and Cinema Studio
Produce branded Marketing Studio ads with avatars, products, hooks, and settings
Analyze video virality with Virality Predictor for hook strength and attention scoring
Convert images to video with start and end frame transitions
Chain with higgsfield-soul-id for consistent face identity across generations
limitations: Cannot train Soul Characters or create identity models (use higgsfield-soul-id instead)
Does not support product photoshoots or marketplace listing cards
Cannot process text-only tasks, chat, or TTS requests
Video duration and resolution vary by model and are subject to schema constraints

use_cases.title: Social media content creation
use_cases.description: A social media manager needs to produce a series of product images and short video clips for multiple platforms. This skill generates on-brand visuals using GPT Image 2 and creates UGC-style videos with Marketing Studio, all without a design team.
use_cases.target_user: Social media managers and content creators

use_cases.title: E-commerce marketing video production
use_cases.description: An e-commerce brand wants to create product demo and unboxing videos for their online store. The skill imports products from URLs, sets up avatars, and generates branded ad videos with hooks and settings through Marketing Studio.

use_cases.title: Video creative testing and optimization
use_cases.description: A growth marketer needs to evaluate multiple video ad variants before launch. Virality Predictor analyzes hook strength, attention retention, and distraction risk for each clip, enabling data-driven creative decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Simple image generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of a cozy coffee shop interior with warm lighting, wooden tables, and a barista at work.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick image creation for a blog post or social media

prompt_templates.title: Video from image with motion
prompt_templates.prompt: Make a video from this photo of a sunset beach scene. Add gentle camera movement and ocean waves motion.
prompt_templates.scenario: Image-to-video conversion for social media content

prompt_templates.title: Branded ad video with product
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a UGC-style ad video for this organic protein bar product. Use a friendly presenter who talks about natural ingredients and post-workout energy.
prompt_templates.scenario: Marketing Studio ad creation for a DTC product

prompt_templates.title: Video virality analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this video ad for hook strength and attention retention. I want to know if the first 3 seconds are strong enough and where viewers might lose interest.
prompt_templates.scenario: Pre-launch creative testing for ad optimization

output_examples.input: Generate an image of a modern tech office with floor-to-ceiling windows and collaborative workspaces.
output_examples.output: A high-resolution image of a bright, airy tech office with floor-to-ceiling windows, natural light streaming in, open collaborative workstations, plants, and modern furniture. Generated with GPT Image 2.

output_examples.input: Make a 12-second product showcase video for this wireless earbud product. Use a clean white background with the product rotating.
output: A 12-second product showcase video showing wireless earbuds in their charging case on a white surface, with smooth 360-degree rotation, soft shadows, and the case lid opening to reveal the earbuds. Generated with Marketing Studio in product_showcase mode.

output_examples.input: Analyze this video creative for hook strength and virality potential.
output_examples.output:
Virality Predictor Results:
Overall score: 72/100
Peak hook: 81% attention at 2 seconds
Sustain score: 65%
Strongest region: Visual Cortex (product reveal)
Risk: Slight attention dip at seconds 5-7 during transition

Open full report: https://app.higgsfield.ai/apps/virality-predictor?resultJobId=xyz789

best_practices: Use --wait flag with generate commands so the CLI blocks until completion and outputs the result URL automatically.
Pass media files as local paths; the CLI handles uploads automatically. No need to pre-upload before generation.
Run higgsfield model list before selecting a model to verify the latest available models and their display names.
anti_patterns: Do not run create and wait as separate steps. Use --wait in a single command instead of the two-step create then wait pattern.
Do not pass --hook_id and --setting_id to models that do not support them. Check the model schema with higgsfield model get first.
Do not use both an ad reference and hook/setting in the same generation. These two approaches are mutually exclusive.

faq.question: What is the default image model?
faq.answer: GPT Image 2 is the default image model. It handles high-fidelity general generation, graphic design, UI, banners, typography, and on-image text.

faq.question: How do I generate a video from a photo?
faq.answer: Use --start-image flag with models like Seedance 2.0 or Kling 3.0. Pass your photo as a local file path and the CLI will auto-upload it.

faq.question: Do I need to install something first?
faq.answer: Yes, install the higgsfield CLI first, then run higgsfield auth login to authenticate. The skill will guide you through bootstrap steps.

faq.question: Can I reuse a face across multiple generations?
faq.answer: Yes, chain with higgsfield-soul-id to train a Soul Character first. Then pass the returned reference id using --soul-id to text2image_soul_v2 or soul_cinematic.

faq.question: What is Virality Predictor and how do I use it?
faq.answer: Virality Predictor (brain_activity) analyzes a finished video and returns scores for hook strength, attention, retention, and distraction risk. Pass a video file with --video flag, no prompt needed.

faq.question: How do I create a branded ad with my product?
faq.answer: Use Marketing Studio. Import a product from a URL or create one from images. Optionally pick an avatar, hook, and setting, then generate with marketing_studio_video or marketing_studio_image.
